title: Impacts of nature-inclusive urban development on well-being and fairness perceptions
authors: Sophus O. S. E. zu Ermgassen

data_scope.study_site: Qunli New Town (East area), Harbin, Heilongjiang, China

core_conclusion: Even an ecologically successful nature-inclusive urban development can produce uneven social outcomes. In Qunli New Town, former agriculturalists reported a sharp happiness decline (OR=0.15), while happiness gains tracked perceived social (OR=1.30) and aesthetic (OR=1.53) value of green space, not housing. Fairness perceptions diverged by settlement wave: original residents saw the change as economically fairer (OR=1.93) but ecologically less fair (OR=0.54) than newcomers, driven by rural reference points and loss of customary landscape access turned into exclusive club goods.
why_it_matters: Rare empirical evidence of state-led green gentrification in the global south; shows ecological success can coexist with social inequity, a direct caution for Global Biodiversity Framework Target 12; reveals reference-dependent fairness invisible to income metrics; argues social impact assessments must differentiate settlement waves and keep green space a public good.
why_nature: Links nature to human well-being and distributional justice; globally relevant to the 2022 Global Biodiversity Framework; extends gentrification theory to state-led urbanization via reference points and socio-ecological displacement; rigorous sequential mixed-methods design (42 interviews + survey N=1,018 + ordinal logit).
added_value_to_my_research: Map the club-good divide with 2SFCA/R2SFCA and calibrate decay/quality weights against survey-based fairness
Scale single case to multi-city green-gentrification comparison via multi-source data fusion (RS, POIs, mobile, social media)
Explainable ML (SHAP) on context-dependent well-being to surface non-linear reference-point thresholds
Link ecological displacement to objective mental-health/activity outcomes, bridging access and care demand
Encode 'differentiate settlement waves' as equity-aware/agent-based green-space allocation optimization
